Collect samples of Cannabis or Cannabis products from Cannabis facilities that have already been tested by Cannabis laboratories in amounts deemed sufficient by the Commission;
Remove identifying characteristics from and randomize such samples; and
Provide each Cannabis laboratory with a sample for analysis.
A Cannabis laboratory that receives a sample from the Commission shall perform such quality assurance tests upon the sample as the Commission may require. Such tests may include, without limitation:
Screening the sample for pesticides, heavy metals, chemical residues, herbicides, growth regulators and microbial analysis;
A potency analysis to test for and quantify the presence of the following cannabinoids:
THC;
Tetrahydrocannabinolic acid;
Cannabidiol;
Cannabidiolic acid;
Cannabinol; and
Such other quality assurance tests that the Commission may require.
If the Commission establishes a program pursuant to paragraph (A) of this section, the Commission shall adopt regulations necessary to carry out the program. Such regulations include:
Must require each Cannabis laboratory to perform a random laboratory assurance check at least once every six (6) months but not more frequently than once every three (3) months; and
May modify the procedures and requirements set forth in this section if the Commission determines that advances in science necessitate such a modification;

As used in this section, "random laboratory assurance check" means the evaluation of the performance of a Cannabis laboratory in conducting quality assurance tests upon a sample if required by the Commission under the program established pursuant to paragraph (A) of this section; and

Consumption within a Cannabis facility. The Commission may adopt regulations prescribing the requirements and standards for the consumption of Cannabis and Cannabis products within a Cannabis facility.

section.heading: Certain Employees and Persons Exempt from Prosecution

para.text: A member or employee of the Commission is exempt from tribal prosecution for any act during the performance of their duties where the member or employee would:
Possess, deliver, or produce Cannabis;
Aid and abet another in the possession, delivery or production of Cannabis;
Performs any combination of the acts described in paragraph (A)(1) and (A)(2) of this section; or
Performs any other criminal offense in which the possession, delivery or production of Cannabis is an element.
The persons described in this section must ensure that the Cannabis described in this section is safeguarded in a secure location.

In addition to the provisions of paragraph (A) of this section, no person may be subject to Tribal prosecution for constructive possession, conspiracy, or any other criminal offense solely for being in the presence or vicinity of the Cannabis in accordance with the provisions of this title.
As used in this section, "Cannabis" includes, without limitation, Cannabis products.

It is the public policy of the Tribe that contracts related to the operation of Cannabis facilities under this title should be enforceable, and no contract entered into by the licensee as permitted pursuant to such a license, or by those who allow property to be used by a licensee as permitted pursuant to such a license, shall be deemed unenforceable on the basis that the actions or conduct permitted pursuant to the license are prohibited by federal law.

The Courts of the Upper Mattaponi Tribe shall have jurisdiction over any party to a contract related to the operation of Cannabis facilities under this title for the purposes of enforcement of such contract.

section.heading: Petition to Determine if Criminal History is Disqualifying

para.text: The Commission shall develop and implement a process by which a person with a criminal history may petition the Commission to review the criminal history of the person to determine if the person's criminal history will disqualify the person from obtaining a license pursuant to this title.

Not later than ninety (90) days after a petition is submitted to the Commission pursuant to paragraph (A) of this section, the Commission shall inform the person of the determination of the Commission of whether the person's criminal history will disqualify the person from obtaining a license. The Commission is not bound by its determination of disqualification or qualification and may rescind such a determination at any time.

The Commission shall provide instructions to a person who receives a determination of disqualification to remedy the determination of disqualification. A person may resubmit a petition pursuant to paragraph (A) of this section not earlier than six (6) months after receiving instructions pursuant to this section if the person remedies the determination of disqualification.

A person with a criminal history may petition the Commission at any time, including, without limitation, before obtaining any education or paying any fee required to obtain a license from the Commission.
A person may submit a new petition to the Commission not earlier than two (2) years after the final determination of the initial petition submitted to the Commission.

The Commission shall impose a fee of up to $50.00 upon the person to fund the administrative costs in complying with the provisions of this section. The Commission may waive such fees or allow such fees to be covered by funds from a scholarship or grant.
The Commission shall post on its internet website:
The requirements to obtain a license from the Commission; and
A list of crimes, if any, that would disqualify a person from obtaining a license from the Commission.
The Commission shall request the criminal history record of a person who petitions the Commission for a determination pursuant to paragraph (A) of this section.
A person who petitions the Commission for a determination pursuant to paragraph (A) of this section shall not submit false or misleading information to the Commission.

The Chief of the Tribe, or their designee, may negotiate one or more intergovernmental agreements to efficiently coordinate the cross-jurisdictional administration of the laws of the Tribe and the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia relating to Cannabis. The agreements may include, without limitation, provisions relating to:
Criminal and civil law enforcement;
Regulatory issues relating to the possession, delivery, production, processing or use of Cannabis or Cannabis products;
The administration of laws relating to taxation;
Any immunity, preemption, or conflict of law relating to the possession, delivery, production, processing, or use of Cannabis or Cannabis products; and
The resolution of any disputes between a tribal government and the state, which may include, without limitation, the use of mediation or other nonjudicial processes.
Any agreement negotiated pursuant to this section shall not take effect unless and until the agreement is approved by the Council through the legislative process in the form of a bill or resolution.
An agreement entered into pursuant to this section shall:
Provide for the preservation of public health and safety;
Ensure the security of Cannabis establishments;
Establish provisions regulating business involving Cannabis that passes between tribal lands and nontribal lands in Virginia; and
Be negotiated in good faith, which shall respect and protect tribal sovereign immunity.

There shall be no Cannabis facility license, other than a license for a Cannabis laboratory or Cannabis wholesaler, issued to any person or entity other than an entity wholly owned by the Tribe and organized under Tribal law.

Entities wholly owned by the Tribe that operate pursuant to a Cannabis facility license shall not comingle revenues generated by the Cannabis facility with other tribal funds. All revenue should be held in a bank account separate from the Tribe's bank account and managed and invested separately from tribal funds.

If the Commission becomes aware that a licensee or Cannabis agent has violated, is violating or is about to violate any provision of this title or any regulation adopted pursuant thereto, the Commission may transmit the details of the suspected violation, along with any further facts or information related to the violation, to the appropriate law enforcement agency.

If any person other than the Commission becomes aware that a licensee or Cannabis agent has violated, is violating or is about to violate any provision of this title or any regulation adopted pursuant thereto, the person may file a written complaint with the Executive Director specifying the relevant facts. The Executive Director shall provide the complaint to the Commission. The Commission shall review each such complaint and, if the Commission finds the complaint not to be frivolous, may transmit the details of a suspected criminal violation, along with any further facts or information derived from the review of the complaint to the appropriate law enforcement agency.

The Commission and its employees shall reasonably cooperate with the appropriate law enforcement agency in the performance of any investigation by responding in good faith to reasonable requests for data, documents, or information.
The Commission may conduct, or cause to be conducted, an investigation of any suspected violation of this title or applicable law.

If the Commission transmits the details of a suspected violation to a law enforcement agency pursuant to Title 28, 700, the law enforcement agency shall investigate the matter in accordance with its policies and procedures.

If the Commission proceeds with disciplinary action against anyone under its jurisdiction, the Commission shall serve a complaint upon the respondent either personally, or by registered or certified mail at the address of the respondent that is on file with the Commission. Such complaint must be a written statement of charges and must set forth in ordinary and concise language the acts or omissions with which the respondent is charged. The complaint must specify the sections and regulations which the respondent is alleged to have violated, but must not consist merely of charges raised in the language of the sections or regulations. The complaint must provide notice of the right of the respondent to request a hearing. The Chair of the Commission may grant an extension to respond to the complaint for good cause.
Unless granted an extension, the respondent must answer within twenty (20) days after the service of the complaint. In the answer the respondent:
Must state in short and plain terms the defenses to each claim asserted;
Must admit or deny the facts alleged in the complaint;
Must state which allegations the respondent is without knowledge or information to form a belief as to their truth. Such allegations shall be deemed denied;
Must affirmatively set forth any matter which constitutes an avoidance or affirmative defense; and

May demand a hearing. Failure to demand a hearing constitutes a waiver of the right to a hearing and to judicial review of any decision or order of the Commission, but the Commission may order a hearing even if the respondent so waives his or her right.

Failure to answer or to appear at the hearing constitutes an admission by the respondent of all facts alleged in the complaint. The Commission may take action based on such an admission and on other evidence without further notice to the respondent. If the Commission takes action based on such an admission, the Commission shall include in the record which evidence was the basis for the action.

The Commission shall determine the time and place of the hearing as soon as is reasonably practical after receiving the respondent's answer. The Commission shall deliver or send by registered or certified mail a notice of hearing to all parties at least ten days before the hearing. The hearing must be held within forty-five (45) days after receiving the respondent's answer unless an expedited hearing is determined to be appropriate by the Commission, in which event the hearing must be held as soon as practicable.
Before a hearing before the Commission, and during a hearing upon reasonable cause shown, the Commission shall issue subpoenas and subpoenas duces tecum at the request of a party.
The testimony of any material witness residing within or without the jurisdiction of the Tribe may be taken by deposition in the manner provided by the Upper Mattaponi Rules of Civil Procedure.
At all hearings before the Commission:
Oral evidence may be taken only upon oath or affirmation administered by the Commission.
Every party has the right to:
Call and examine witnesses;
Introduce exhibits relevant to the issues of the case;
Cross-examine opposing witnesses on any matters relevant to the issues of the case, even though the matter was not covered in a direct examination; and
Offer rebuttal evidence.
The respondent cannot be compelled to testify without their consent;

The hearing need not be conducted according to technical rules relating to evidence and witnesses. Any relevant evidence may be admitted and is sufficient in itself to support a finding if it is the sort of evidence on which responsible persons are accustomed to rely in the conduct of serious affairs; and
The parties or their counsel may by written stipulation agree that certain specified evidence may be admitted even though such evidence might otherwise be subject to objection.

The Commission may take official notice of any generally accepted information or technical or scientific matter within the field of Cannabis, and of any other fact which may be judicially noticed by the Upper Mattaponi Trial Court or High Court. The parties must be informed of any information, matters, or facts so noticed, and must be given a reasonable opportunity, on request, to refute such information, matters, or facts by evidence or by written or oral presentation of authorities, the manner of such refutation to be determined by the Commission.
Affidavits may be received in evidence at any hearing of the Commission in accordance with the following:

The party wishing to use an affidavit must, not less than ten (10) days before the day set for hearing, serve upon the opposing party or counsel, either personally or by registered or certified mail, a copy of the affidavit which the party proposes to introduce in evidence together with a notice as provided in paragraph (C)(3) of this section;

Unless the opposing party, within seven (7) days after such service, mails or delivers to the proponent a request to cross-examine the affiant, the opposing party's right to cross-examine the affiant is waived and the affidavit, if introduced in evidence, must be given the same effect as if the affiant had testified orally. If an opportunity to cross-examine an affiant is not afforded after request therefore is made in accordance with this paragraph, the affidavit may be introduced in evidence, but must be given only the same effect as other hearsay evidence; and
The notice referred to in paragraph (C)(1) of this section must be substantially in the following form:

The accompanying affidavit of [here insert name of affiant] will be introduced as evidence at the hearing set for the … day of the month of … of the year … [Here insert name of affiant] will not be called to testify orally and you will not be entitled to question [here insert name of affiant] unless you notify the undersigned that you wish to cross-examine [here insert name of affiant]. To be effective your request must be mailed or delivered to the undersigned on or before seven days from the date this notice and the enclosed affidavit are served upon you.

_______________
(Party or Counsel)

At least three (3) members of the Commission shall be present at every hearing, and they shall exercise all powers relating to the conduct of the hearing and shall enforce all decisions with respect thereto.
The proceedings at the hearing must be reported by either a stenographic or phonographic reporter.

section.heading: Limitations on Communication
section.text: After the Commission has initiated a hearing, the members of the Commission shall not communicate, directly or indirectly, in connection with any issue of fact, with any person or party, nor, in connection with any issue of law, with any party or the party's representative, except upon notice and opportunity to all parties to participate.

section.heading: Amended or Supplemental Pleadings
section.text: The Commission may, before submission of the case for decision, permit the filing of amended or supplemental pleadings and shall notify all parties thereof, and provide a reasonable opportunity for objections thereto.

para.text: If any person in proceedings before the Commission disobeys or resists any lawful order or refuses to respond to a subpoena, or refuses to take the oath or affirmation as a witness or thereafter refuses to be examined, or is guilty of misconduct during the hearing or so near the place thereof as to obstruct the proceeding, the Commission may certify the facts to the Upper Mattaponi Trial Court.
The Trial Court shall thereupon issue an order directing the person to appear before the Trial Court and show cause why the person should not be punished as for contempt.

The Trial Court order and a copy of the statement of the Commission must be served on the person cited to appear. Thereafter, the Trial Court has jurisdiction of the matter, and the same proceedings must be had, the same penalties may be imposed and the person charged may purge himself or herself of the contempt in the same way as in the case of a person who has committed a contempt in the trial of a civil action before the Trial Court.

Within sixty (60) days after the hearing or service of the complaint, whichever is later, the Commission shall render a written decision on the merits which must contain findings of fact, a determination of the issues presented, and the penalty to be imposed, if any. The Commission shall thereafter make and enter its written order in conformity to its decision as follows:
No member of the Commission who did not hear the evidence may vote on the decision;
The affirmative votes of a majority of the whole Commission are required to impose any penalty;
Copies of the decision and order must be served on the parties personally or sent to them by registered or certified mail; and
The decision is effective upon such service, unless the Commission orders otherwise.

Provided that a petition for judicial review of the decision and order has not been filed, the Commission may, upon motion made within ten (10) days after service of a decision and order, order a rehearing before the Commission upon such terms and conditions as it may deem just and proper as follows:

The motion may only be granted except upon a showing that there is additional evidence which is material and necessary and reasonably calculated to change the decision of the Commission, and that sufficient reason existed for failure to present the evidence at the hearing of the Commission;

The motion must be supported by an affidavit of the moving party or his or her counsel showing with particularity the materiality and necessity of the additional evidence and the reason why it was not introduced at the hearing;
Upon rehearing, rebuttal evidence to the additional evidence shall be permitted; and
After rehearing, the Commission may modify its decision and order as the additional evidence may warrant.
If the Commission finds that a licensee or agent has violated a provision of this title or any regulation adopted pursuant thereto, the Commission may take any or all of the following actions:
Limit, condition, suspend or revoke the license;
Impose a civil penalty in an amount established by regulation for each violation; and
Tax the costs associated with the investigation and hearing against the licensee or agent.
Any person aggrieved by a final decision or order of the Commission may obtain a judicial review thereof in the Upper Mattaponi Trial Court.

The judicial review must be instituted by filing a petition with the Trial Court within twenty (20) days after the notice of the final decision or order. The petition must set forth the order or decision being appealed and the grounds or reasons why petitioner contends a reversal or modification should be ordered. A petition may not be filed while a motion for rehearing or a rehearing is pending before the Commission. If a motion for rehearing or rehearing is pending before the Commission, the time to file a petition with the Trial Court shall be tolled until the motion for rehearing or rehearing is decided.
Copies of the petition must be served upon the Commission and all other parties of record, or their counsel of record, either personally or by certified mail.
The Trial Court, upon a proper showing, may permit other interested persons to intervene as parties to the appeal or as friends of the court.
The filing of the petition does not stay enforcement of the decision or order of the Commission, but the Commission itself may grant a stay upon such terms and conditions as it deems proper.
Upon written request of the petitioner, the complete record on review, or such parts thereof as are designated by the petitioner, must be prepared by the Commission.
The complete record on review must include copies of:
All pleadings in the case;
All notices and interim orders issued by the Commission in connection with the case;
All stipulations;
The decision and order appealed from;
A transcript of all testimony, evidence and proceedings at the hearing;
The exhibits admitted or rejected; and
Any other papers in the case.
The original of any document may be used in lieu of a copy thereof.
The record on review may be shortened by stipulation of all parties to the review proceedings.

The record on review must be filed with the Trial Court within thirty (30) days after service of the petition for review, but the Trial Court may allow the Commission additional time to prepare and transmit the record on review.
The Trial Court may, upon motion therefor, order that additional evidence in the case be taken by the Commission upon such terms and conditions as the Trial Court deems just and proper as follows:

The motion shall not be granted except upon a showing that the additional evidence is material and necessary and that sufficient reason existed for failure to present the evidence at the hearing of the Commission;

The motion must be supported by an affidavit of the moving party or his or her counsel showing with particularity the materiality and necessity of the additional evidence and the reason why it was not introduced in the Commission hearing;

Rebuttal evidence to the additional evidence shall be permitted. In cases in which additional evidence is presented to the Commission, the Commission may modify its decisions and orders as the additional evidence may warrant and shall file with the Trial Court a transcript of the additional evidence together with any modifications of the decision and order, all of which become a part of the record on review;

Judicial review shall be confined to the record prepared by the Commission in accordance with this Chapter. The filing of briefs and oral argument must be made in accordance with the Trial Court's rules; and

The Trial Court may affirm the decision and order of the Commission, or it may remand the case for further proceedings or reverse the decision if the substantial rights of the petitioner have been prejudiced because the decision is:
In violation of this title;
In excess of the authority or jurisdiction of the Commission;
Made upon unlawful procedure;
Unsupported by any evidence; or
Arbitrary or capricious or otherwise not in accordance with law.
Any party may appeal the decision of the Trial Court to the Tribe's High Court in accordance with the High Court's rules and procedures for appeals.

The judicial review afforded in this title is the exclusive method of review of the Commission's actions, decisions, and orders in disciplinary hearings against a licensee. Review of actions, decisions, and orders of the Commission relating to the denial of a license is pursuant to regulations issued by the Commission.
